items.q: Why do astronomers infer the existence of dark matter?
items.correct: Visible matter alone cannot account for galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing.
items.misconception: Dark matter has been directly observed in telescopes.
items.random_wrong: Because the universe looks dark at night.
items.why: Students think dark matter has been seen. It's INFERRED from gravitational effects on visible matter and light - galaxies rotate too fast for visible mass alone, implying unseen matter contributes most gravity.

items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.

items.correct: A unit of DISTANCE - the distance light travels in one year.
items.why: Students hear 'year' and assume time. Light year is distance (about 9.46 trillion km) - it's a useful astronomical scale, not a measure of when something happened.
It's Axis tilt. “Distance from Sun” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: On the summer solstice in the UK, day is:
items.why: It's Longest. “Shortest” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the tilt of Earth's axis in degrees (nearest whole)?
items.why: The real figure is 24. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
